---
title: "The Toxicity Premium: Understanding the Economics of Content Moderation"
author:
- name: Hauke Roggenkamp [![](../misc/orcid.png){width=1.8%}](https://orcid.org/0009-0005-5176-4718)
  email: Hauke.Roggenkamp@unisg.ch
  orcid: 0009-0005-5176-4718
  corresponding: true
  affiliations:
    - name: Institute of Behavioral Science and Technology, University of St. Gallen
      address: Torstrasse 25
      city: St. Gallen
      country: Switzerland
      postal-code: 9000
- name: Jonas Görgen [![](../misc/orcid.png){width=1.8%}](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6263-1532)
  email: Jonas.Goergen@unisg.ch
  orcid: 0000-0002-6263-1532
  corresponding: true
  affiliations:
    - name: Institute of Behavioral Science and Technology, University of St. Gallen
      address: Torstrasse 25
      city: St. Gallen
      country: Switzerland
      postal-code: 9000
date: now
date-format: long
format: 
  pdf:
    # classoption: twocolumn
    include-in-header: 
      text: |
        \usepackage{titling}
        \usepackage{sectsty}
        \usepackage{lettrine}
        \usepackage{amsmath}
        \renewcommand{\LettrineTextFont}{\normalfont}
        \allsectionsfont{\rmfamily}
        \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\rmdefault}
        \pretitle{\begin{center}\LARGE\rmfamily}
        \posttitle{\end{center}}
        \predate{\begin{center}\rmfamily}
        \postdate{\end{center}}
        \setlength{\parindent}{0.25in}
        \setlength{\parskip}{0pt}
        
# format: 
#   html:
#     embed-resources: true
#     theme: cosmo
#     code-line-numbers: true
toc: false
number-sections: false
fig-cap-location: top
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
bibliography: ../literature/_references.bib
nocite: |
  @*
abstract: |
  We examine the economic tensions underlying content moderation decisions on social media platforms. Specifically, we investigate a fundamental paradox: toxic content that creates negative externalities for society may drive higher user engagement while simultaneously deterring advertisers concerned with brand safety. Analyzing over 53,000 headline variations from randomized controlled trials on Upworthy—a platform that created clickbait content specifically optimized for social media sharing—we find that headlines with higher likelihood of rejection by content moderators generate significantly greater user engagement, with each standard deviation increase in rejection likelihood corresponding to approximately 2% higher click-through rates. To further explain this "toxicity premium," we employ two complementary Digital In-Context Experiments. The first experiment tests whether varying levels of feed toxicity reveal a theorized non-monotonic relationship with user engagement. The second uses an incentive-compatible design to map advertisers' bidding responses to content toxicity. By studying these competing dynamics, we provide insights into the economic mechanisms driving platform content moderation decisions and contribute to the growing discourse on digital governance, demonstrating how platforms must navigate trade-offs between engagement optimization, societal welfare, and brand safety concerns.
---

```{r packages_CRAN}
#| warning: false

options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org")) 

if (!requireNamespace("groundhog", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("groundhog")
}

pkgs <- c("magrittr", "data.table", "stargazer", "ggplot2",
          "remotes", "osfr", "knitr", "stringr", "broomExtra",
          "dotenv", "usethis", "devtools",
          "quanteda", "quanteda.textstats",
          "lme4", "gtsummary")

groundhog::groundhog.library(pkg = pkgs,
                             date = "2025-01-01") # R-4.4.1

groundhog::groundhog.library(pkg = "favstats/peRspective",
                             date = "2025-01-01") # R-4.4.1

rm(pkgs)
```

```{r plot_layout}
#| warning: false

layout <- theme(panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),
                legend.key = element_rect(fill = "white"),
                panel.grid.major.x = element_line(colour = "grey", 
                                                  linewidth = 0.25),
                axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
                panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
                axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "#000000", 
                                                  linewidth = 0.5),
                axis.line.y.left = element_blank(),
                plot.title = element_text(size = rel(1))
)
```

```{r plot_colors}
c_orange <- "#F0941F"
c_teal   <- "#196774"

scale_color_custom_d <- function() {
  scale_color_manual(values = c(c_orange, c_teal))
}

scale_fill_custom_d <- function() {
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(c_orange, c_teal))
}
```


\lettrine[lines=3]{S}{ocial} media platforms thrive on user-generated content as their fundamental resource. When _Meta_'s users share a billion stories (disappearing posts) [every day](https://web.archive.org/web/20240531061745/https://www.facebook.com/business/news/stories-can-do-it), each post serves multiple strategic purposes: First, users themselves tend to stay engaged to monitor responses to their posts. Second, more content allows platforms to better match other users with relevant posts, leading to increased time spent on the platform [@AridorEtAl_2024]. Third, a rich content ecosystem attracts new users to the platform, as they are more likely to find communities and conversations that interest them. This growing user base and enhanced engagement makes the platform more valuable to advertisers who compete for user attention through paid placements, creating a self-reinforcing cycle where content generation drives both user growth and revenue.

In addition to facilitating content production, the core of a platform's business is to effectively distribute content to maximize its consumption. Due to the unprecedented scale of content production, social media platforms cannot expose all users to all available content. Instead, they rely heavily on recommender systems to curate personalized feeds of content. These algorithms are crucial to the platform's business model: Unlike traditional media outlets where editors select content for a general audience, recommender systems analyze individual user behavior to identify and promote content most likely to drive engagement and ad revenue. The better these systems perform at matching users with relevant content, the more time users spend on the platform and the more valuable each advertising slot becomes.

Despite selecting potentially relevant content per user, platforms also filter out content that should not be displayed to any user. This filtering has become increasingly crucial as online hate and harassment has emerged as a societal threat, affecting 48% of Internet users [@ThomasEtAl_2021]. As platforms cannot control content production directly, they typically set rules that define which type of content shall (not) be displayed and have developed automated tools to take action against toxic content such as hate speech, threats, and identity attacks. However, content moderation creates both direct and indirect costs for platforms. The direct costs stem from developing these detection algorithms and employing human moderators to evaluate flagged content. More significantly, field experimental evidence indicates substantial indirect costs: The removal of toxic content—including hate and misinformation that creates negative externalities for society [@Beknazar-YuzbashevEtAl_2022, p. 8]—leads to _reduced_ content consumption and thus lower advertising revenue. This economic impact is further illustrated by YouTube's _Adpocalypse_, where the platform's adoption of stricter content moderation led content creators to shift toward subscription-based models on alternative platforms like Patreon [@AndresRossiTremblay_2023]. Notably, this shift didn't eliminate toxic content but merely redistributed it, resulting in increased toxicity on Patreon [which would be a problem for us if Marketing would consider a general equilibrium as important.]

The relationship between toxic content and platform revenue is further complicated by what triggered these moderation changes in the first place: advertiser preferences. The Adpocalypse began when major brands discovered their ads appearing alongside controversial content and departed from YouTube due to brand-safety concerns. This pattern has repeated across platforms: When Elon Musk announced plans to relax Twitter's content moderation policies in 2022, GroupM, the world's largest media buyer, classified Twitter as a "high-risk platform," leading many luxury brands like Balenciaga to pause or cease their advertising [@AndresRossiTremblay_2023, p. 1]. These reactions are economically rational: research shows that advertisers bid more when they know their ads will appear in brand-safe contexts [@AdaEtAl_2022], and premium brands particularly depend on high-quality content environments for successful campaigns [@ShehuEtAl_2021]. This suggests that platforms face not just a binary trade-off between societal welfare and engagement [@Beknazar-YuzbashevEtAl_2022, @Jimenez_2021], but must navigate a complex optimization problem: While toxic content may drive user engagement and time spent on the platform, it can simultaneously reduce advertising revenue if brands perceive the platform as unsafe for their ads.

The present research investigates how content toxicity affects the dual-sided market dynamics of social media platforms. We theorize a non-monotonic relationship between toxicity and user engagement, where moderate levels of toxicity may increase engagement through heightened emotional arousal and controversy, while extreme toxicity likely decreases engagement by making the platform experience aversive. This builds on recent theoretical work showing how harmful content can simultaneously decrease user utility while increasing engagement through complementarity effects [@Beknazar-YuzbashevJiménez-DuránMateusz_2024]. Simultaneously, we expect advertiser willingness-to-pay to be a strictly decreasing function of content toxicity due to brand safety concerns. Through two linked experiments using simulated Twitter feeds, we aim to identify the qualitative properties of these relationships: (1) examining whether varying levels of feed toxicity reveal an inverse U-shaped pattern in user engagement, and (2) mapping the shape of advertisers' bidding response functions. While the specific intersections between these functions may not directly generalize to real-world platforms, understanding their qualitative properties (particularly the presence of non-monotonicity in user engagement and the shape of advertiser responses) provides  insights into the economic mechanisms underlying platform content moderation decisions.

```{r simulate_data}
sim <- data.table(toxicity = seq(from = 0, to = 100, by = 1),
                  engagement = NA,
                  wtp = NA,
                  revenue = NA)

sim[, engagement := 0.9 - 1.2 * (toxicity - 50)^2]
sim[, wtp := 1 - 0.2 * toxicity - 0.8 * toxicity^2]
sim[, revenue := engagement * wtp]

sim[, engagement := pmax(engagement, 0.3)]
# sim[, engagement := (engagement - min(engagement))/(max(engagement) - min(engagement))]
# sim[, wtp := (wtp - min(wtp))/(max(wtp) - min(wtp))]
# sim[, revenue := (revenue - min(revenue))/(max(revenue) - min(revenue))]
```

```{r}
sim[, engagement := 0.5 + 0.5 * (1 - ((toxicity - 50)/70)^2)]
sim[, engagement := pmax(engagement * (1 - 0.5 * (toxicity/100)^4), 0.05)]
sim[, wtp := 1 - 0.1 * (toxicity/100) - 0.5 * (toxicity/100)^2]
sim[, revenue := 1.33 * engagement * wtp]
```

```{r}
max_revenue <- sim[which.max(revenue), toxicity]
max_engagement <- sim[which.max(engagement), toxicity]
p0 <- ggplot(data = sim,
       mapping = aes(x = toxicity)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank()) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  xlab("Toxicity") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, max_revenue, max_engagement, 100),
                     limits = c(0, 100))
```

```{r}
p1 <- p0 + 
  geom_line(aes(y = engagement)) +
  ylab("Engagement")
p1
```

```{r}
p2 <- p0 + 
  geom_line(aes(y = wtp)) +
  ylab("Ad Spent")
p2
```

```{r}
p3 <- p0 + 
  geom_line(aes(y = revenue)) +
  ylab("Revenue")
p3
```


# Studying Toxicity

In many applications, “toxicity” is defined as rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable messages that are likely to make someone leave a discussion. This definition follows from the one used by [Google’s Perspective](https://perspectiveapi.com/) algorithm, which is widely used in the industry and serves as a benchmark in academic studies [@AridorEtAl_2024].

Our focus on toxic content, rather than alternative forms of harmful content such as misinformation or spam, is motivated by societal, theoretical, and methodological considerations. From a societal perspective, toxic content poses an increasingly urgent challenge as social media becomes more central to public discourse and daily life. The prevalence of online toxicity has been linked to concerning phenomena such as declining mental health outcomes [@AllcottEtAl_2020; @BraghieriEtAl_2022] and in extreme cases, offline violence stemming from online hate speech [@MuellerSchwarz_2020; @MuellerSchwarz_2023; @LewandowskiRobertsonDiResta_2024]. These effects exemplify the negative externalities of toxic content: while individual users may derive benefits from posting inflammatory material, the costs are borne broadly across society, affecting both direct targets and third parties who weren't involved in the original interaction [@Beknazar-YuzbashevEtAl_2022]. The presence of these externalities, combined with platforms' complex incentives around content moderation, makes toxic content a particularly important focus for both researchers and policymakers.

From a measurement perspective, toxicity presents a uniquely suitable construct for empirical investigation. Unlike misinformation, which requires extensive fact-checking and can be highly context-dependent, or spam, which typically manifests as a binary classification, toxicity can be reliably measured along a continuous scale using established tools such as the Perspective API, LLMs or alternative tools [see @AntypasEtAl_2024]. This continuous nature of toxicity allows for more nuanced manipulation and analysis of its effects. 

Moreover, toxic content creates a particularly interesting tension within platform business models: while it may drive user engagement, it simultaneously risks deterring advertisers due to brand safety concerns. This dynamic is less pronounced with spam, which is universally unwanted by all stakeholders, or misinformation, where the platform incentives are more politically charged and complex. 

Toxicity's effects can also be observed within a single browsing session, enabling controlled experimental studies without the need for longitudinal observation. Furthermore, studying toxic content allows for the creation of realistic experimental stimuli without raising ethical concerns about spreading actual misinformation. 

<!--
The research most closely related to our work is @Beknazar-YuzbashevEtAl_2022, who conducted a six-week field experiment examining how toxic content affects user engagement. Using a browser extension that automatically hid toxic content on major social media platforms, they demonstrated that lowering exposure to toxicity reduced content consumption on Facebook by 23% and ad consumption on Twitter by 9.2%. Their field experimental approach offers high ecological validity by studying user behavior in natural social media environments.
-->


# The Moderation Gap and Brand Safety

Building @AridorEtAl_2024, we focus here on the platform’s choice of *moderation intensity* and how it interacts with both user engagement and advertiser concerns. To this end, we model a platform as choosing a a personalized subset of content $\textbf{x}_i$ from the total pool of posts to show a user $i$ so as to maximize its revenue-weighted engagement:

$$
\max_{\{\textbf{x}_i\}_i \subset \cup_j \textbf{x}_j^p} \sum_i \alpha(x_i)\,t_i(x_i),
$$

\noindent where $t_i(\textbf{x}_i)$ is the total time that user $i$ spends consuming the curated content $\textbf{x}_i$. $\alpha(\textbf{x}_i)$ represents the monetary gains the platform gets per unit of time spent from showing $\textbf{x}_i$ to $i$. As we model an advertising-based business model, this parameter equates to the share of posts that are advertisements and the average price paid for the advertisement. Notably, this simple framework abstracts from potential social costs or negative externalities of harmful content as well as from any impact such content may have on advertiser willingness to pay.


## 1. Extending the Framework to Include Moderation

To capture the tradeoff between engagement and the costs (and benefits) of content moderation, let $M$ denote the platform’s *moderation intensity*, i.e., how aggressively it removes or downranks content deemed harmful or toxic. When $M$ is higher, the pool of permissible (or algorithmically boosted) content is narrower, but less toxic such that its overall quality or “brand safety” is improved.

We can introduce a simple function to capture how moderation affects the platform’s profit:

$$
\Pi(M) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_i \alpha(M)\,t_i(M)}_{\text{Revenue}} \;-\; \underbrace{C(M)}_{\text{Moderation Costs}} \;-\; \underbrace{E(M)}_{\text{Social Costs}},
$$

\noindent where:

1. **$\alpha(M)$**: The average revenue per unit of time, which depends on the level of moderation $M$. Absent brand safety concerns, $\alpha$ might be constant or even *decreasing* in $M$ (since removing certain provocative content can reduce engagement). However, *with* brand safety concerns, $\alpha$ may *increase* in $M$, as advertisers are willing to pay more if they perceive the content environment to be safer.

2. **$t_i(M)$**: The total time user $i$ devotes to the platform as a function of $M$. Typically, greater moderation can remove some “attention-grabbing” but harmful content, so $t_i(M)$ may initially decrease with $M$. However, extremely low moderation can deter users if the platform becomes rife with harmful content. Thus, $t_i(M)$ may exhibit a non-monotonic relationship with $M$.

3. **$C(M)$**: The platform’s *direct costs of moderation*, including the labor cost of human moderators, the development of algorithmic detection tools, and the potential overhead of appeals processes. These costs typically *increase* in $M$.

4. **$E(M)$**: The *social costs* of harmful content—such as hate speech, harassment, or misinformation. While the platform itself may not directly internalize these harms in the baseline model, society at large incurs them (e.g., negative mental health impacts on users, or offline polarization). As $M$ increases, $E(M)$ declines because less harmful content circulates. In private decision-making, $\frac{dE}{dM}$ may be undervalued or unrecognized. Hence, these costs qualify as negative externalities.


## 2. The Moderation Gap

### 2.1. No Brand Safety Concerns

First, consider the case in which advertisers do not respond to harmful content (i.e., brand safety is not a salient concern). The platform then chooses $M$ to solve:

$$
\max_M \Bigl[\sum_i \alpha\,t_i(M) - C(M)\Bigr],
$$

\noindent where $\alpha$ is a constant unaffected by content quality. Because the externalities $E(M)$ are borne largely by third parties (or society at large) rather than the platform, $\frac{dE}{dM}$ does not enter the platform’s maximization. Consequently, the platform sets moderation $M^*$ that equates the marginal cost of moderation to the marginal revenue benefit in terms of engagement:

$$
\alpha \,\frac{d t_i(M)}{dM} \;=\; \frac{d C(M)}{dM}.
$$

This *privately optimal* level of moderation may be far below the *socially optimal* level $M^o$, which would solve:

$$
\alpha \frac{d t_i(M)}{dM} \;=\; \frac{d C(M)}{dM} \;+\; \frac{d E(M)}{dM}.
$$

Because $\frac{dE(M)}{dM} < 0$ (i.e., higher moderation lowers social costs), $\frac{d^2 t_i(M)}{dM^2} > 0$, and because the social costs are not considered by the platform, $M^* < M^o$. We refer to the difference $M^o - M^*$ as the *Moderation Gap*—the shortfall in moderation intensity relative to what would be welfare-maximizing for society.

### 2.2. Introducing Brand Safety Concerns

Now suppose advertisers *do* respond negatively to harmful content, fearing reputational damage from their ads appearing next to toxicity. This means $\alpha$ is *not* constant but instead depends on $M$. A simple functional form could be:

$$
\alpha(M) \;=\; \alpha_0 + \beta M
$$

\noindent where $\beta > 0$ indicates that advertisers pay more when they see higher moderation (i.e., a “safer” environment). The platform’s problem becomes:

$$
\max_M \Bigl[\sum_i \alpha(M)\,t_i(M) - C(M)\Bigr].
$$

Because higher $M$ now *raises* the per-user revenue rate $\alpha(M)$, the platform internalizes part of the external harm: it wants to avoid alienating advertisers and losing revenue. In effect, brand safety concerns *shrink* the Moderation Gap:

$$
\frac{d \alpha(M)}{dM} > 0 
\quad \Rightarrow \quad 
\alpha(M)\,t_i(M) \text{ is no longer independent of }M.
$$

Graphically, this brand-safety-aware revenue curve rises in $M$ rather than remaining flat or decreasing. By more fully internalizing the downside of harmful content, the platform’s privately optimal moderation level $M^s$ moves closer to $M^o$. Indeed, if brand safety concerns align perfectly with the broader social costs of harmful content (i.e., if $\beta\,\Delta M \approx \Delta E$ for relevant ranges of $M$), the platform may end up implementing an intensity of moderation that is nearly socially optimal.


## 3. Discussion and Implications

This extended framework highlights how brand safety concerns can partially or wholly offset the externalities that platforms might otherwise ignore. When advertisers demand a safer environment—and punish insufficient moderation by reducing ad spending—the platform has a private incentive to raise its moderation level. From a policy standpoint, it is crucial to recognize that relying solely on brand safety concerns may not eliminate the Moderation Gap if:

1. **Advertisers’ Perceptions Differ from True Externalities:** If advertisers primarily respond to *public controversies* rather than to more pervasive but less visible harms, the platform’s moderation choices may remain misaligned with social welfare.
2. **User Engagement Offsets:** Higher moderation can reduce certain types of highly “engaging” content. If user time $t_i(M)$ drops sharply when moderation is increased, the platform might still hesitate to adopt the socially optimal $M$.
3. **Heterogeneous Advertisers:** Some advertisers (e.g., smaller or niche brands) may be less sensitive to toxic content, reducing the overall brand-safety penalty.

Overall, when advertisers take brand safety seriously, the platform’s profit function increasingly internalizes some social costs of harmful content, raising moderation toward the socially optimal level. Absent those advertiser concerns, the platform’s privately optimal moderation remains lower, perpetuating the Moderation Gap. This tension underscores why exogenous pressure—whether from advertisers, regulators, or users themselves—can be decisive in aligning private moderation choices with broader societal goals.
